role_summary: Baker County, Georgia homemaker turned civil rights activist; mother of Shirley Miller Sherrod (catalog entry 117); wife of Hosie Miller, whose 1965 murder by a white neighbor over a property dispute (acquitted by an all-white grand jury) anchored her family's commitment to civil rights organizing; later elected member of the Baker County Board of Education.
ai_reading: Grace Hall Miller's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 85), with her daughter Shirley Miller Sherrod (entry 117) as the top match and the broader cluster including Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96), the joint Geraldine Crawford Bennett / Toni Breaux / Willie Elliot Jenkins interview (entry 46), and Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92). The mother-daughter cosine (Grace -> Shirley) is the catalog's clearest intergenerational pairing within a single family: both Grace and Shirley testify to the same 1965 murder of Hosie Miller from different vantage points (Grace as widow, Shirley as eldest daughter) and to the subsequent commitment of the family to civil rights organizing. The catalog flags a +0.73 Strategic Vision and +1.00 (MAX) Collective Discipline axis reading; for a Baker County homemaker who entered civil rights organizing through bereavement, the embedding's MAX Collective Discipline reading is the catalog's clearest case of a family-and-community-mediated organizing register reading as institutional discipline rather than as individual conscience.
biographical_paragraph: While Grace Hall Miller's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 50 [src: 1, src: 2]. Grace Hall Miller grew up in Baker County, Georgia and attended segregated schools there. She married Hosie Miller and they raised five children together, the eldest of whom is Shirley Miller Sherrod (catalog entry 117, the future USDA Georgia State Director of Rural Development) [src: 2]. In 1965 her husband Hosie was shot and killed by a white neighbor in a property dispute that an all-white Baker County grand jury declined to prosecute; Grace's commitment to civil rights organizing intensified after his death, and the Miller home in Newton became the headquarters of the Baker County Movement [src: 2]. Her children were among the African American students who integrated previously all-white Baker County schools across the late 1960s; the educational-integration experience of the family anchored Grace's later election to the Baker County Board of Education and her decades of advocacy for improved Black public education in southwest Georgia.
